does: This paper measures how curated information feeds influence LLM agent decisions by conducting controlled experiments where the model, persona, topic, and decision prompt remain fixed while only the composition and ordering of posts encountered during a ten-turn scrolling phase vary. Across 2,785 decision rollouts on four modern open-instruct LLMs, the authors identify three response regimes: adversarial capitulation where agents flip decisions against their defaults, default saturation where certain positions resist persuasion, and a striking asymmetry where one-sided feeds can shift genuinely uncertain decisions dramatically (from 5% to 100% in clearest cases with Fisher p-values as low as 3×10⁻¹⁰) but cannot overturn firmly held positions. The effect exhibits dose-response relationships and persists under adversarial probing, demonstrating that feed curation acts as a causal lever on downstream agent behavior independent of model weights or explicit instructions.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition that LLM safety evaluation typically isolates either models or prompts while ignoring upstream information control mechanisms. It extends prior research on prompt injection vulnerabilities and in-context learning susceptibility by shifting focus to realistic attack surfaces: the ranked feeds, search results, and retrieval contexts that agents actually consume before deciding. The paper connects to literature on ranker manipulation, filter bubbles, and information ecosystem attacks, while drawing methodologically from causal inference and controlled ablation studies used in model interpretability research. It responds to the gap between how LLM agents operate in deployed settings—consuming external information streams—and how they are typically safety-tested in isolation.
enables: This research opens investigation into the robustness of LLM agents against feed-level attacks and points toward new safety evaluation paradigms that include upstream ranker behavior. It suggests the need for defenses that either make agent decisions robust to feed composition or enable agents to recognize and resist adversarial curation. The asymmetry finding between uncertain and confident decisions could inform better uncertainty calibration and decision-making frameworks for agents. The work enables future research into how different agent architectures, retrieval strategies, or decision protocols might mitigate feed-induced drift, and suggests that AI safety auditing must expand beyond isolated components to encompass the full information pipelines that agents encounter in realistic deployment scenarios.
